data.title: Upstart: All Eyes On Management Execution In The Storm (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Upstart (NASDAQ:UPST) had looked like it was coming into high gear, but the tariff escalations have cast considerable doubt on the sustainability of that progress. Long-time investors may remember that this stock faced great cyclicality following the pandemic and the rise of interest rates. While management remains confident that their improvements have set the company up to thrive even in future downturns, investors will be looking at how the company performs during these uncertain times. The company has accelerated its top-line growth and management continues to guide for GAAP profitability over the coming quarters. With the stock returning to recent highs, I suggest fading this rally. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

UPST Stock Price

I last covered UPST in March, where I upgraded the stock to a buy rating as I became a believer in the turnaround. The stock has risen 50% since then. That belief is facing a test as the risks of a recession have risen due in part to tariff uncertainty.

I am surprised to see UPST trading near recent highs, even as uncertainty has increased dramatically.

UPST Stock Key Metrics

UPST is a financial tech company which has utilized artificial intelligence to lower its loan origination platform. The success of its models has enabled it to automatically approve an increasing percentage of applications over time. The goal is to achieve a product that can approve a wider range of applicants at comparable or higher rates of return, enabling bank customers to invest more capital than would have otherwise been possible through traditional credit models.

The latest quarter saw the company continue its strong recovery, with originations growing 89% YoY to $2.1 billion.

The company has increased its disclosures to break out the credit profile of its borrowers - we can see below that its recent growth has not come at the expense of its “super prime” originations.

The company delivered 67% YoY revenue growth to $213 million, crushing guidance of $200 million. I note that investors likely still view the stock as having a great deal of cyclically, making the sizable beat less significant.

Contribution margins contracted to 55%, below guidance of 57%. While management noted that this reflects faster growth, I note that this is a potential negative point given that it was just in the prior quarter that management was confidently explaining expectations for contribution margins to remain resilient.

Adjusted EBITDA of $43 million crushed guidance of $20 million (but I again emphasize that investors are unlikely to care so much about the magnitude of these beats, given the presumed cyclicality).

UPST continued to make progress on GAAP profitability and was within striking distance of breakeven.

The company ended the quarter with $600 million of cash versus $1.4 billion of debt. It also carried $815 million of loans on the balance sheet, though the amount not classified as R&D has been declining. I note that it isn’t immediately clear what are the criteria for what is considered an R&D loan and what is not - we are merely taking management’s word here.

Looking ahead, management guided for the company to generate 76% YoY revenue growth to $225 million. Management also slightly increased full-year revenue expectations and reiterated expectations to generate positive GAAP net income for the full year. I wouldn’t be surprised if the company continues to “beat and raise,” but again, the challenge here centers around convincing investors that the growth is “different this time” and will not disappear like it did in 2021 and 2022.

On the conference call, management noted that their “ability to adapt to changing macroeconomic conditions is miles better than it was just a couple of years ago.” However, without any way to really quantify that besides back-tested results, I suspect that such commentary will not win over many investors. Management discussed their new one-year agreement with Walmart’s (WMT) OnePay which makes their lending products available to Walmart customers. Management noted that WMT does have “some share” in the deal but that they “feel very good about the economics and the agreement.” My guess is that investors should lean on the side of conservatism, as improving name brand recognition might end up being the most significant outcome from this partnership, but, of course, we can hope for more.

At the company’s AI Day, management, among other things, touted their improving ability to fully automate loan approvals, which has helped increase their conversion rates.

They notably did not give any financial guidance, which is arguably prudent given their recent track record. I note that at a subsequent investment conference, management may have inadvertently given some rough expectations with their long-term profit expectations, with CEO Girouard discussing a future where the company was generating “$10 billion in revenue and $2.5 billion in EBITDA.” Ironically, this might be viewed as somewhat of a disappointment to some investors given that it seems to imply that the company would be operating at only a 25% EBITDA margin even after a 10x gain in revenues, but again I note that this was not explicitly mentioned as a formal financial target.

Is UPST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, UPST was trading hands at just around 7x sales.

Following the aforementioned commentary regarding profitability, I am adjusting my long-term net margin assumption to 22%, down from 35%. That places the current valuation at around 31x earnings. I see the valuation settling at around 12x to 15x earnings once the company matures, assuming it is still required to occasionally hold loans on its balance sheet. With the company looking poised to sustain around 20% top-line growth for many years as it takes market share, the stock may eventually grow into its valuation, but it may take as many as 4 years. With the macro environment looking rather uncertain, I do not find it appropriate for the stock to be pricing in so many years of growth.

UPST Stock Risks

UPST is highly exposed to market volatility. While it is very-well possible that the company has fully perfected its algorithms such that loan default rates would not be materially impacted by a weakening economy, I do not yet have the confidence to make such an assertion given that it was less than a handful of years ago that the company had to re-work its algorithms. If the company can successfully navigate through a downturn, then it might be able to earn some credibility in the form of a re-rating. I, however, expect this to take quite some time and with perhaps several cycles required, as it is admittedly difficult to believe that this company would be able to significantly revamp such a long-standing industry. This might cause the stock to continually trade at low valuations, which might hurt near term returns given the not-so-cheap earnings multiple. Management will need to execute on driving both top and bottom-line growth and sustaining the progress - all not such easy tasks for this young company.

UPST Stock Conclusion

UPST is a difficult name to stand by in the current environment, but the company appears to be benefiting from strong fundamental momentum. The next many quarters will show if the company has truly made progress in improving its credit models, and successful execution may help the company sustain this high valuation multiple over the long term. The stock appears to trade at a noted premium to many fintech peers, perhaps due to having a popular retail investor presence, but the elevated macro uncertainty calls that premium into question. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

data.title: What Amazon Needs To Break Out
data.description:
Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN) has a love for round figures. After stabilizing through the pandemic, Amazon traded between $150 to $200 till 2022, fluctuating between the support and resistance. Through the rate cut pressures of 2022-23, the trading range became $100 to $150. Almost a buy around $100 and exit around $150 trade. In the mega cap rally of 2023 to 2024, the trading range shifted up to $150 to $200. Since then, it is hovering around the $200 mark, with a potentially shifted trading range between $200 and $250 now.

The predictable and slow-moving nature of Amazon made a buy below $200 during the recent tariff sell-off a no-brainer. I had recommended a Buy at around $167 (another roundish figure two-thirds of the way down in the $150 to $200 range). That call, in hindsight, was in a zone when buying anything would have been a great move. Of course, for a defensive stock, Amazon corrects more generously than rallies from breakout points (not rebounds - that's usually strong). So, buying Amazon on crashes is easier than many other names, even among the defensive group. Combining that with strong fundamentals, it is far easier to bet on Amazon than anything else when the markets are crashing. It feels safe and comfortable at a time when catching other falling knives becomes arduous. Amazon, after all, has not seen the explosion many other big tech names have in the past. A generous correction is often the zone for Amazon - a relatively comfortable buy with cushioned downsides backed by fundamentals that have not truly reflected in prices at a time when many other big tech names have soared.

The graphs below show how Amazon has not been able to break away even amongst the defensive tech names since the pandemic. Also, a graph showing how a blind pick from the defensive techs would have done during the tariff lows. Amazon has not outperformed other defensive tech plays significantly in both the short-term and long-term timeframes.

No wonder, my Buy call has turned out successful. A 31% return in 2–3 months is just about the best Amazon can do going by its price action history. With macro pressures in play and an optimal reward reaped in, should investors book profits? My recommendation was a fundamental long-term Buy, but what do fundamentals say now (with emphasis on why Amazon doesn't break out meaningfully and often plays by trading ranges)? More importantly, perhaps what can propel Amazon to the next trading range of $250-$300? These are some questions I will evaluate in this analysis that will help take a call on what to do with Amazon now.

Why Was I Bullish Amazon?

A lot of factors I was bullish about should be seen from the lens of what prices Amazon was trading at back then. However, revenue growth in Q1 2025 has only improved from when I went long. Even taking FX drags into considerations, the net sales show a 9% YoY jump. Operating income has jumped 20% YoY to $18.4b. Net income per share has seen a robust increase too, from $0.98 to 1.59.

A lot of these improvements suggest structural tailwinds that should support Amazon fundamentally going ahead, not a quarter of execution excellence alone. AWS sales grew 17% to $29.3b, with operating income climbing ~23% to $11.5b. Some of the margin improvement is likely due to workforce optimization and balancing data center expansion rates. But there are structural levers supporting AWS margins too. Use of Trainium and Graviton chips could lower compute cost per unit going forward as well. A strategic shift to longer-term contracts should also help capacity planning. Most importantly, I will watch the continued topline growth in AWS, which potentially points to revival of cloud demand and stronger AI-driven surge. I expect this tailwind to continue to expand faster than expected as more foundational model hosting, training and inference workloads increase. Management did talk about the Tranium2 and the Bedrock Model developments that will support multi-billion AI revenue streams.

Improvements in Amazon's headline numbers were also helped by the e-commerce resilience in the face of tariff pressures. However, this is an area that could fluctuate. Temporary stocking up before tariff regimes settle could be moving the margins and topline here. I see Amazon making the right moves to brace for any tariff impact that are structurally supportive. Such as regional fulfillment centers that could cut shipping costs and times and cushion any international shipment drags. The move to private label products is already ongoing to reduce reliance on Chinese imports. And of course, like most other trade impacted supply chains, restructuring supply chain towards India, Vietnam and Mexico should also reduce impacts. Then there is AI-driven forecasting to reduce tariff impacts through operational discipline and expansion of its logistics moat outside internal use (increasing efficiencies of scale in addition to expanding revenue streams). Given all the support, tariffs will still be a drag and the e-commerce leg will have to rely on executional and innovative plays to keep the segment performing well and then grow.

Ad revenues are the fastest growing segment and expected to hold healthy margins too. Q1 2025 saw a continuation of the trend with a 19% YoY growth in topline. This stream will likely see benefits from AI tool rollouts that can auto-generate creatives and widen the ad buyer base. Amazon Primes' ad rollouts have started to sink into the topline as well, and upcoming quarters should see the impact of a stronger ad uptake there. Amazon's ad outlets are far more diverse anyway than Meta or Google's with Search, DSP, Video and Fire TV giving Amazon a launchpad capable of being resilient to ad spending cuts.

Overall, Q1 2025 trends do show structural supports. Management guidance is not too pessimistic, with Q2 sales expected to land between $159b-$164b. Management did highlight caution around tariffs and their ripple effects. Free cash flow is already under pressure from heavy capex spending around logistics and AI. Not a problem per se, given the reinvestments are in high-growth areas. But capex and cost discipline could be watched more closely now. From what I was expecting from Amazon in April 2025, I think overall there are no major red flags in the long-term story. In fact, strides made since support development is core pillars of my thesis. I am enthused and confident about AWS growth in the mid-teens and a growing Ad revenue. However, retail may need to optimize margins as pressure from tariffs will weigh on the e-commerce arm. Long-term capability build-outs in AI, logistics, rural delivery and Project Kuiper suggest steady expansion of the Amazon ecosystem and moat. But short-term pressures from competition (cloud), tariff escalations and a macro slowdown could keep the lid on Amazon shares till the dust settles.

What Needs to Happen for Amazon

For Amazon to move into the next trading range of $250-$300, a lot of further developments need to show up still. And, of course, near term drags should stabilize. AWS is definitely at the core of the thesis. Stronger enterprise adoption of AI/cloud tools showing further structural gains after the recent rebound is a key re-rating trigger. More AI native services and a demonstration of pricing power in the face of competition will be another key area to watch out for. A growth level of 20-25% in AWS and if sustained could push Amazon beyond the $250 barrier now. As of the next few quarters, that is not expected.

What will also help is AI and productivity payoffs becoming more tangible. Custom chips and Bedrock investments are starting to trickle into real revenues. But markets will like to see how AI monetization is shaping in the cloud, and also in logistics and ads segments. Some public use cases and stories like Microsoft's Copilot will help. This story will likely again need a few quarters to play out.

The current uncertainty of retail will also need to improve. Higher ad margins and product targeting, retail dominance without pricing pressure and new monetization avenues will be keenly watched. Again, this is not likely to show strength in the near term. The near term is more about how well Amazon is able to defend the business in the face of slowing macro and tariff drags.

Till these measurable outcomes show up, markets will likely watch out for capex control and FCF growth. What does not boost Amazon's chances of tipping over $250 is the current macro backdrop of higher rates for longer, consumer spending slowdown and consolidation of tech valuations after a run-up.

Amazon's valuations at ~14x EV to forward EBITDA is not expensive, but a far cry from the ~10-11x around where I had initiated a Buy call. I don't see immediate support for a re-rating towards the 15x plus zone, which is historically the high zone for Amazon. Re-ratings can occur only when multiple levers fire in tandem, i.e. AWS growth accelerating toward 20%+, margin expansion in retail and meaningful AI monetization. Without that trifecta, a move beyond 15x EV/EBITDA may be difficult to sustain. This supports the idea that Amazon could consolidate between $200 and $250 in the near term. Price targets beyond that range will need either earnings beats or a shift in macro sentiment. Until then, the current valuation reinforces my view that booking profits after a 30%+ move is prudent, and re-entering below $200 offers a better risk-reward.

Waiting on Amazon A Prudent Option

After analyzing all the competing forces, it is clear that Amazon is not plagued by any long-term fundamentals scare. However, the short term does not look conducive for further upside at this point. Those who were able to enter at $167 may want to book profits, looking at the immediate lack of triggers for an upside. $250 is likely to be a good resistance. On the other hand, I don't expect Amazon to break the $200 support anytime soon as well.

Amazon is a Hold for the near term. Any decline towards or below $200 could be a re-entry point. Or some of the re-rating triggers discussed kicking in. Long-term Amazon investors have nothing to fear, but should be prepared for a few quarters of consolidation. Any crash on Amazon is its strength territory. It may sound a given for all stocks, but Amazon's crashes have historically seen the fastest and strongest rebounds, making it a most comfortable "buy on crash" stock. For now, book profits and wait. Or wait if you are a long-term investor, not affected by a year's consolidation.

data.title: U.S. Labor Force Participation Drops To Lowest Level Since 2022
data.description:
The share of Americans actively in the workforce hit a new low in June, extending a stretch of workers increasingly exiting employment or the job search.

In June, 62.3% of Americans aged 16 or older were working or actively looking for work, the lowest share since December 2022, according to the latest US Bureau of Labor Statistics data. Before the COVID-19 pandemic upended the US labor market in 2020, the labor force participation rate had not dipped to its latest low since 1977.

Meanwhile, continued unemployment claims - which count workers that already filed for initial unemployment benefits and are still seeking them - held at 1.96 million for the week ending June 21, the highest level since 2021, according to the US Labor Department. Taken together, the claims and labor force participation data may signal that some are becoming discouraged over finding work and withdrawing from the labor force, said James Knightley, chief international economist with ING.

"If it continues, it can become a constraint on future growth as it would limit the supply side capacity of the economy to meet the needs of any pick up in demand," Knightley said in an email to S&P Global Market Intelligence. "There is also a sense that it can lead to more personal hardship and more strain on government social services, with fewer people feeling the benefits of economic growth."

Much of the drop in labor force participation comes from the youngest and oldest workers in the workforce - those between 16 and 19 years old and those who are 55 or older. Labor force participation for both groups lags its pre-pandemic levels, while the rate for so-called "prime-age" workers between 25 and 54 has risen.

Economists have pointed to retirements, generous severance after a layoff, younger workers' focus on higher education and general disillusionment with the labor market as possible reasons for the declining share of workers in the labor force in the youngest and oldest groups.

Unemployment falls, hiring rises

Lower labor force participation likely contributed to a slight decline in the unemployment rate to 4.1% from 4.2% in May. The rate has hovered near 4% for more than a year.

Additionally, employers added 147,000 jobs in June, a faster clip than the 110,000 expected, according to estimates compiled by Econoday.

Still, job gains in June were concentrated mainly in healthcare, private education and state and local government.

"Employment growth outside of those marquee industries has been anemic at best, and the duration of unemployment for the typical unemployed worker seeking a job continues to creep up," said Cory Stahle, an economist at the Indeed Hiring Lab, in a July 3 report. "There are real weaknesses in the market - including concentrated job gains, slowing wage growth, and falling participation - that have persisted for months, and there are scant signs of those concerns fading anytime soon."

Investors largely viewed the latest labor market data as justification for the US Federal Reserve to extend its pause on lowering benchmark interest rates.

The odds of no change in the benchmark federal funds rate at the July 29–30 meeting of the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee shot above 93% shortly after the labor market report, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UnitedHealth Remains Largest U.S. Insurer By Market Cap After 40% Hit In Q2 2025
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) continued to be the largest US insurer by market capitalization during the second quarter, even as it experienced a historic tumble as investors balked at earnings figures.

In an analysis of the 20 largest US insurers by market cap, UnitedHealth had by far the largest decrease, falling 40.9% in the second quarter and 39.6% year-over-year. The managed care leader was beset by turmoil after it pulled its 2025 earnings guidance.

Of the eight market cap decreases of more than 5% in the analysis, four were by managed care insurers. Of the six increases, five were by property and casualty insurers.

Even with the sharp decline, UnitedHealth remained the largest US insurer by market cap, at $283 billion. There is a significant gap between UnitedHealth and the second-largest, The Progressive Corp. (PGR), which had $156.44 billion in market cap.

Health insurers catch a chill

UnitedHealth has had a difficult time of late, beginning with the assassination of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson on Dec. 4, 2024. This continued in May when Andrew Witty abruptly stepped down as UnitedHealth Group's CEO. UnitedHealth announced May 13 that former CEO Stephen Hemsley returned to that role and that it was pulling its 2025 guidance.

The subsequent stock market slide continued May 15 after The Wall Street Journal reported UnitedHealth was facing a US Department of Justice probe into possible Medicare fraud.

UnitedHealth did not respond to a request for comment regarding its second-quarter performance.

Fellow health insurers Centene Corp. (CNC) and Elevance Health Inc. (ELV) posted declines of more than 10% during the second quarter, with Centene down 10.3% and Elevance falling 10.8%. Humana Inc. (HUM) and The Cigna Group (CI) decreased by a more modest 7.6% and 1%, respectively, during the same period.

The S&P 500 insurance index was up 19.55% year-over-year by the end of the second quarter, outpacing the broader market, as the S&P 500 index rose 15.16% during the same period.

Managed care insurers also had a shaky start to the third quarter after Centene announced July 2 that it had pulled its 2025 earnings guidance, sending its stock plummeting 40.37% for the day. Other managed care insurers were impacted, too, including UnitedHealth and Elevance, which fell 5.7% and 11.5%, respectively.

President Donald Trump's legislative push, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, appears poised to receive congressional approval and could cause millions of Americans to lose access to Medicaid, news of which sent the managed care space into another free fall during the first week of the third quarter. Medicaid generally involves private companies such as UnitedHealth and Centene administering health insurance plans using government funds.

Corebridge leads market cap growth

Corebridge Financial Inc. (CRBG) had the highest growth in market cap from the previous quarter among the 20 companies in the analysis. Corebridge, which recently announced it was removing a "headwind to earnings" through its reinsurance transaction that covers all variable annuities in its individual retirement business, increased its market cap by 11.2%, growing to $19.51 billion.

The company now ranks as the 19th largest public insurer by market cap in the US.

Markel Group Inc. (MKL) reported the second-largest percentage increase in market cap with a gain of 6.1%. The insurer is sixth in year-over-year growth, increasing its market cap 23.7% by the end of the quarter.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Waste Connections: A Solid Business, But The Stock's Already Expensive
data.description:
Introduction

Waste Connections (NYSE:WCN) is a very successful North American solid waste roll-up with a solid moat in secondary markets, stable pricing, and a steady M&A machine that has been successful at adding size without imploding. The numbers are true to the story — margins are widening, cash flow is strong, and the recent acquisitions are well integrated. The problem is the price. At 25x EBITDA and 75x GAAP earnings, the stock already prices in a lot of optimism. Renewable natural gas (RNG) and recycling are interesting long-term tailwinds, but too small to be worth the multiple today. It is a good business, but at full price. As I do not see an asymmetric upside, I rate it a Hold.

Business Overview

Waste Connections is not a glamorous business, but it's a solid one. It's a garbage collection and disposal firm that operates landfills, recycles, and in more and more cases, captures methane and turns it into renewable natural gas. It does that in most of the United States and a chunk of Canada. It ranks third in size compared to Waste Management and Republic, but does not necessarily play in the same sandbox. Waste Connections has focused on the mid-sized suburban and rural markets, where it's harder for local players to achieve scale and where pressure on prices isn't quite so intense. It enjoys sticky, multi-year municipal contracts, with 25% of total contracts having durations of over a year (page 101). And in the long run, that has provided them with fairly secure niche positioning with good pricing power and relatively stable cash flow.

M&A Strategy and Pricing Power

The narrative of the last ten years has been one of steady revenue growth, with plenty of assistance from M&A. That trend didn't abate. In 2024 alone, they did 24 transactions, bringing in around $750 million of revenue annually. Some were small contracts, some a bit more material, like Royal Waste in New York and a handful of assets from Waste-Away in Indiana, but none were company-changing. That's the idea, anyway. These are tiny bolt-ons that fit into current operations seamlessly. Waste Connections has been making these types of deals for a long time and appears to have developed a genuine internal strength around integration. To their credit, I haven't seen them falter in any material manner, which is unusual for repeat acquirers.

Market Positioning and Regulatory Tailwinds

Pricing has also been a component of their success. They've been able to pass through price increases of about 7% for the last two years, which is more than inflation and reflects true pricing power in their underlying markets. A lot of this is because of their model of not entering highly populated urban areas where there is more pricing competition and labor churn. They instead saturate smaller cities and towns where they've had long-term relationships with municipalities, many of which would not be willing to switch providers unless something was seriously amiss.

They’ve also been getting a boost from regulatory tailwinds. In several states, new extended producer responsibility bills have been signed into law. These amount to essentially forcing manufacturers to pay more of the cost of recycling packaging and material. The company is in a good position to capitalize, as they already own and operate recycling plants and are seeing increasing volumes as a result.

Recycling isn’t yet a good business on a standalone basis because it’s not that profitable, but if volumes keep rising and the regulatory momentum continues, that might change. And then there's RNG, or methane captured in landfills and converted into fuel. Waste Connections is betting big on these initiatives, attempting to capitalize on landfill gases and entering a quickly growing business in low-carbon fuels. RNG is a tiny percentage of revenue now, but is a growth business. With government subsidies and because RNG is eligible for premium pricing, the company is already seeing early signs of high-margin payback. It's too early to call this a game-changer, but the foundation being laid has the potential to become material in the next two years.

Financial Performance

Finances remain healthy at the top line. 2024 revenue of $8.92 billion was more than 11% above 2023 due to acquisitions and price increases. Adjusted EBITDA margins expanded to 32.5%, a point better than the previous year. That sort of margin expansion in this environment, inflation, wage inflation, fuel price risk, is noteworthy and reflects beneficial operating leverage. That being said, reported GAAP net income fell to $617.6 million due in large part to a massive $613 million non-cash write-down of landfill closure and remediation. I wince at write-downs of this size, but it doesn't appear to be a recurring issue, and cash generation is robust. Adjusted for the write-down, net income rose to $1.24 billion, which is the figure most investors care about anyway.

Recent Results and Balance Sheet

First quarter 2025 results continue this good trend. The business recorded $2.23 billion of revenue, 7.5% year-over-year growth, and EBITDA margins still in the 32% ballpark. Pricing and acquisitions were specifically highlighted by management as guiding the trend, which is consistent with recent history. Free cash flow was $332 million for the quarter, and they're carrying a net debt to EBITDA ratio of somewhere in the 2.6x to 2.7x range. That's fairly comfortable for this business, and it provides them with plenty of room to either sustain the pace of acquisitions or return more capital to the shareholders. I suspect they'll do the former, but a special dividend or buyback wouldn't be out of place if deal flow slows or becomes more costly.

Valuation

The real issue here is valuation. The business is good, but the stock isn't cheap. On a trailing GAAP basis, it's more than 75 times earnings. Even on an adjusted basis, the multiple is elevated. EV/EBITDA is in the 25x range, compared to Waste Management (WM) and Republic (RSG) in the 17x to 18x range. Price-to-sales is north of 5.4x. None of this is cheap, and you're definitely paying a growth premium for what is, at the end of the day, a garbage company. A good one, to be sure, but not some kind of hyper-growth SaaS company. The premium might be fair, though, if you're sure of the RNG upside, continued outsized M&A synergies, and sustained pricing strength, but that's a lot to assume, never mind all at once. There's not much margin for error here.

It's the question of whether too much optimism is being factored into the market for these initiatives. The company's 24 transactions in 2024 and expansion into renewable natural gas have certainly conveyed long-term growth opportunity, but perhaps elevated expectations as well. Investors might be projecting early success with RNG and acquisition integration into long-term growth that isn't set in stone. The roughly 4% sell-off in the stock on July 2nd may be exactly that — high expectations colliding with reality. This reinforces my thesis: Waste Connections is a good operator, but today's price doesn't leave much room for disappointment.

Company P/E (GAAP) P/E (Adjusted) EV/EBITDA Price/Sales Revenue (2024E, $B) Adj. EBITDA Margin (%)
Waste Connections 75 ~40–45 25 5.4 8.92 32.5
Waste Management 35 30 17 3.5 21.7 28
Republic Services 34 29 18 3.4 14.6 29

Technical Setup

Yes, the company's history does deserve some premium, and the stock has done well for the last year. The chart shows an uptrend, technicals are decent, and the stock is below major moving averages. RSI is around 60, and that can potentially forecast a short-term pullback or at least some congestion, but momentum is firmly to the upside. I would not short it, even here, and the market does seem willing to give the company the benefit of the doubt about future growth prospects.

Risks

There are risks, of course. Landfilling is highly regulated, and the California gas leak provided a recent reminder of just how fast things can spin out of control. Environmental stewardship is no joke and can ruin cash flow in an instant unless tightly managed. There is also the risk of shoving ever-increasing organics and e-waste out of the landfill burns throughput, placing RNG feedstock in the stretch. Labor-wise, this is still a driver-heavy operation, and continued wage pressure and labor availability can clip margins south, especially if pricing power is starting to decelerate. Oilfield waste is another source of volatility. That component of the business lags drilling activity, which can be strong in booms but weak in busts. Not a monster revenue contributor, but not small either. Most of all, at this price point, any margin misstep or soft quarter on integration can create a much deeper correction than investors expect.

Conclusion and Rating

My conclusion is that Waste Connections is an excellent business that’s probably priced to perfection. The model works, management has executed, and the company has amazing levers for growth going forward. But the stock is fully valued, perhaps a little overvalued, and I don’t believe there’s an asymmetric opportunity here today. If you’re long, I’d hold. If you’re not long, I’d wait for a pullback or a market dislocation that enables you to get in at a better price. It’s not a short, but it’s not a screaming buy either.

Rating: Hold

data.title: Tariff Uncertainty Is Likely To Continue Through Late 2025
data.description:
Key Findings

  • We assume some form of negotiation-based pause for most trade partners on July 9 based on prior policy backtrack. While deals have been reached with the UK, mainland China and Vietnam, other pending arrangements, including those with the EU, Japan and India, remain complicated by country-specific political issues and ongoing Section 232 reviews.
  • In our pessimistic alternative scenario where tariffs revert to higher International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) levels, Market Intelligence economists estimate US GDP declining 0.5% peak-to-trough over the second half of 2025.
  • Tariff-related uncertainty will remain severe throughout 2025, given the ongoing national security-related Section 232 reviews, US federal court reviews of the IEEPA duties and the prospect of other tariff mechanisms.
  • Corporate reactions to this uncertainty are well established, remaining focused on tactical actions including inventory management, limited price increases, cost negotiations with suppliers and tactical reallocation of sourcing.

What is likely to happen on July 9?

The US administration has made contrasting comments regarding whether reciprocal tariffs implemented under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act will be fully resolved on July 9. Within the likely scenarios, the most plausible outcome is a pragmatic mix of tariff pauses for countries that are undertaking positively viewed negotiations, alongside tariff increases for those countries where the administration believes trade negotiations have stalled.

Many, if not most, trade negotiations will continue after July 9, with tariff pauses likely for most trading partners. Nonetheless, following Trump’s statements that he will not extend the July 9 deadline and plans on implementing higher tariffs after that date, S&P Global Market Intelligence believes countries or territories will likely fall into four categories:

  • Countries or territories that obtain or already have reached a provisional trade deal, as the UK did in May 2025.
  • Countries or territories that obtain a framework for a deal that will necessitate further talks, much like mainland China did in June 2025.
  • Countries or territories where trade talks are ongoing, with increased tariffs paused for now to reflect progress achieved in negotiations to date.
  • Countries or territories where trade talks will continue, but tariffs are increased, as the US seeks greater leverage in negotiations.

There are some caveats to keep in mind. Reaching a provisional deal does not mean tariffs will decrease to the existing 10% universal tariff, as they can be set much higher, but trade policy certainty will temporarily. Countries or territories will move between categories over the course of negotiations, with even those with provisional trade deals still facing potential renegotiations depending upon their bilateral relations with the US.

How might the outcome of these tariff talks affect the US economy?

Market Intelligence believes some increases in tariffs are likely during the third quarter of 2025. Due to trade policy uncertainty, the July 2025 US economic outlook does not assume increases in reciprocal tariffs beyond the 10% universal baseline rate. The forecast will be revised in the upcoming months if conditions change.

Even the 10% baseline tariff (combined with other tariffs authorized under the IEEPA and Section 232) has adverse economic implications. Our baseline forecasts core consumer inflation rising to 3.7% in the second half of 2025, keeping the Federal Reserve on pause through December, thus leaving broad financial conditions tighter than would have been the case without tariff changes.

Under this scenario, we forecast slow economic growth and a gradually rising unemployment rate, which reaches 4.7% by mid-2026.

Along with our baseline US outlook, we have analyzed a “pessimistic alternative” scenario — a reset of reciprocal tariffs to the original April 2 levels. A full reset to the April 2 reciprocal tariffs would imply a peak average effective tariff rate of 23%, 5 percentage points higher than our baseline assumption. If financial markets react negatively, as they did in early April, this would generate conditions likely to cause a mild recession.

Our forecasts for the pessimistic alternative show US GDP declining 0.5% peak-to-trough over the second half of 2025 and the unemployment rate rising to a peak of 6.1%, well above our baseline projection of 4.7%. Inflation would also be higher in this scenario, peaking at 4.1% in late 2025 (versus the 2.4% year-over-year rate, last reported for May 2025).

What is our outlook for tariff policy through 2025?

Irrespective of what the US administration decides regarding the outcome of the IEEPA-related tariff decisions on July 9, there will likely be a range of other tariff-related events during the remainder of 2025.

The US will likely undertake tariff actions driven by specific policy demands, most recently shown by Canada rescinding its Digital Services Tax after Trump’s threat to end talks.

In addition, there are seven ongoing Section 232 reviews, covering about 40% of the total US imports. These are based on a wide definition of national security that includes the economic strength of sectors (such as assembly of consumer electronics as well as sourcing of semiconductors). While the US Commerce Department has deadlines to make recommendations that stretch through January 2026, it can act much sooner.

Another tariff-related event that is playing out in 2025 is a pending US federal court case on the use of IEEPA for assigning tariffs.

How are companies responding to tariffs and trade policy uncertainty?

US importers are deploying previously crafted tactics for dealing with tariffs and have become less negative in the latest earnings season.

Inventory build has been a commonly used approach but has been used differently across the supply chain. US manufacturers have added stocks of purchases at a rapid rate, shown by the US manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index for purchased goods.

The changes in inventories further downstream in supply chains including retail are relatively modest given the high financing costs involved. Firms are also pursuing the use of bonded warehousing and storage in free trade zones to reduce the timing risks associated with tariffs.

Price increases are the quickest route to mitigate tariff exposures but carry a political burden. Negotiating lower costs with suppliers, or burden sharing, is the corollary of price increases, and there has been evidence of reduced import prices on shipments from mainland China.

Longer-term, strategic measures involving new, often heavy capital investments — particularly further reshoring of manufacturing — have been put on hold and are unlikely to proceed until greater clarity on tariffs has been established.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QYLD Vs. JEPQ: 2 Reasons To Go With JEPQ
data.description:
QYLD and JEPQ: previous thesis and new developments

I last covered the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (NASDAQ:QYLD) on 4-23-2025. That article was titled “QYLD Vs. RYLD: Embrace The Fear With QYLD”. The analysis rated QYLD as a buy given the extreme volatility at that time (amid the height of the tariff disputes, as you can see from the chart below). Quote:

The implied volatilities (IV) for both the NASDAQ and Russell indices have risen to quite extreme levels. This offers favorable pricing for both RYLD and QYLD’s use of options. The current IV is even more extreme for QYLD’s underlying index, judging by historical records. This makes QYLD even more attractive than RYLD as reflected in QYLD’s higher dividend yield - both compared to RYLD and its own historical averages.

As for the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ), my last analysis dates further back to 1-28-2025. In an article entitled “JEPQ Vs. JEPI: Why We Now Prefer JEPQ”. The article rated JEPQ as buy, also based on a consideration of the volatilities underlying their assets. Quote:

What caught my attention is that JEPQ also yields higher than its own historical average (by 9%) while JEPI yields 17% lower than its historical average. This divergence signals to me that the market is mispricing their volatilities and offers JEPQ at a more favorable return/risk ratio.

Since those publications, I feel it’s a major understatement to say that our macroeconomic landscape has changed materially. The changes in both the trade negotiations and various geopolitical conflicts are the best capsulated in the VIX index. As shown above, the VIX index (aka, the fear index), after surging to extreme levels in March and April, has subdued to the current level of 16.7x. Given the role of option overlay in both QYLD and JEPQ’s strategies, the above change in VIX also changed their return potentials.

Under this context, the thesis of this follow-up article is to explain why I see a better return/risk curve in JEPQ now. In the subsequent sections of this article, I will argue for a better total return potential for JEPQ, when compared to QYLD, due to at least 2 reasons: JEPQ’s lower fees and also its partial use of its holdings on option overlay (vs. full use in the QYLD fund). These considerations have led me to maintain my buy rating on JEPQ and adjust my rating for QYLD to hold.

QYLD and RYLD ETFs: basic information

JEPQ and QYLD are both considered non-traditional equity ETFs as they both employ an option overlay to their holdings, as detailed in their fund descriptions below. Besides this common strategy, they are also both based on the Nasdaq index, as hinted by their names.

QYLD fund description: QYLD seeks yield from the Nasdaq-100 via options premium. Historically, investors came to the Nasdaq for growth, not yield. Enter QYLD, which provides Nasdaq-100 exposure but earns income by selling call options and passes it on to investors net of fees. Covered call ETFs are hardly new, but QYLD was the first to apply it to the Nasdaq-100. Expect a generally less volatile return pattern from QYLD relative to ETFs tracking the Nasdaq-100.

JEPQ fund descriptions: JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF seeks to deliver monthly distributable income and Nasdaq 100 exposure with less volatility. Underlying equity portfolio employs an applied data science approach to fundamental research and portfolio construction. Disciplined options overlay implements written out-of-the-money Nasdaq 100 Index call options that seek to generate distributable monthly income.

JEPQ was incepted in 2022 and quickly attracted a substantial $27.92 billion in assets under management (AUM). In contrast, QYLD has a much longer history and was incepted in 2013. It manages a sizable but far smaller AUM of $8.41 billion when compared to JEPQ. There are good reasons behind JEPQ’s rapid AUM growth. To start, it features a lower expense ratio of 0.35%, a very competitive level among actively managed funds. In contrast, QYLD carries a higher expense ratio of 0.60%.

Next, I will detail another even more fundamental reason that makes me prefer JEPQ, especially under the current conditions.

JEPQ vs. QYLD: exposure in focus and return prospects

As just mentioned, both funds are based on the Nasdaq stocks and employ an option overlay to their holdings. As an example, the following table shows QYLD ETF's top holdings. As of July 2, 2025, the list is dominated by the major Nasdaq tickers such as NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) (which leads the portfolio with a 9.61% allocation), followed by Microsoft Corp (MSFT) at 9.15%, and Apple Inc. (AAPL) at 7.95%. The top holdings of JEPQ are very similar.

However, this is a key distinction in their investment strategy. JEPQ invests up to 20% of the portfolio in equity-linked notes (ELNs), while the rest is invested in a basket of Nasdaq stocks that the fund selects based on its proprietary algorithm and fundamental research. In contrast, QYLD uses 100% of its portfolio in its option overlay. As you can see from the chart above, QYLD holds a short NASDAQ Call Option with an expiration date of July 18, 2025. This option has a notional exposure of about $8.8 billion as of this writing, closely matching the net asset of $8.4 billion for the fund.

In my experience, the use of a larger portion of the holdings to cover options is a sound strategy when the volatility is high. Thus the pricing environment is favorable for options. However, as mentioned upfront, the volatility has drastically subdued in the past 2~3 months and I expect the VIX index to hover at moderate levels in the near future judging by the current geopolitical landscape.

When/if VIX indeed stays moderate, QYLD’s use of 100% of its assets to cover options would entail two disadvantages at least. First, as just said, its options were sold at relatively low prices, resulting in lower income generation capability. Second, the upside potential of its underlying holdings is more restricted in the case of a rising equity market (or more precisely, a rising Nasdaq market). Past studies (see this paper for an example) have suggested that the overall equity market’s positive perform and lower VIX are positively correlated.

Other risks and final thoughts

The above arguments are supported by historical data (i.e., since the inception of JEPQ). To wit, the chart below illustrates the total return performance of JEPQ and QYLD in the past three years. As seen, JEPQ (represented by the orange line) has significantly outperformed QYLD with a total return of 64.58% (vs. QYLD’s total return of 36.51%). The period in the past 3 years was marked by an overall uptrend for the Nasdaq stocks. And JEPQ superior performance is largely because of its partial use of holdings on option overlay, which makes its upside appreciation potential less severely capped. Given its lower fees and also the current conditions, I see large odds for JEPQ’s outperformance to continue.

To keep the analysis balance, QYLD is a solid fund in its own right – I am rating it as a hold and not a sell after all. It offers a well-round package for income-oriented investors. In particular, QYLD offers significantly higher dividend yields compared to JEPQ. To wit, QYLD’s TTM dividend yield is 13.47%. This is about 18% higher than JEPQ's TTM dividend yield of 11.45% in relative terms, a non-negligible boost of current income for those who need it. Also, as actively managed funds, JEPQ also exhibits a remarkably higher turnover rate of 168%, in comparison to QYLD's much lower turnover of 21%. A higher turnover rates tend to correlate with higher trading frictions and also tax headwinds.

All told, this comparison between JEPQ and QYLD is motivated by the change of drastic change of the VIX index and geopolitical landscape in the past 2~3 months. These changes have led me to see a better total return potential in JEPQ for at least two reasons: its lower fees and its partial use of the underlying assets on the option overlay. Given my outlook for moderate VIX in the near future, these reasons lead me to expect continued outperformance from JEPQ, resulting in a reiteration of my buy rating on JEPQ and a downgrade of QYLD to hold.

data.title: Dell's AI Orders Are Surging - Thanks To Nvidia
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Activity for rack-scale server shipments shot up more than 2x in the opening quarter of this year, as per data from IDC, with the entire server market reaching a record $95.2B in the first quarter.

The record activity in server sales lines up with order data from Dell Technologies’ (NYSE:DELL) earnings report last month that showed the backlog orders regarding AI servers skyrocketed to $14.4B in Q1, single-handedly almost eclipsing the entire year’s worth of AI server order backlog from FY25 last year. The demand that Dell and other AI server vendors are seeing at the moment is a win-win situation for most vendors, especially as Nvidia’s Grace-Blackwell server shipments ramp up throughout the year.

Unfortunately, markets chose to focus on the big EPS miss in Dell’s Q1, a rare occasion by the Texas-based technology company and possibly, one of the largest EPS misses since the pandemic.

In my view, Dell’s strong order activity should warrant praise from investors and points to a robust growth ramp in forward revenue. Dell looks quite attractive at 13x forward earnings, and I reiterate my Buy rating on Dell.

Dell’s Surge In AI Server Orders Is Significant

When I last wrote about Dell at the end of last year, I addressed a key issue that management had tried to call out when attempting to soothe investors' nerves after the Q3 FY25 earnings report.

Here is an excerpt from my previous post on Dell:

One of the reasons for the marginal deceleration that management gave on the Q3 earnings call is “the unpredictability of the AI shipments.” This was after management had already hinted at expecting some sequential deceleration in Q3 on the previous Q2 earnings call after stating their implications for AI server revenues being “down quarter-over-quarter.”

Going through management’s prior comments and pairing it with the performance in the recent Q1 FY26 earnings report, I can see that over time the unpredictability of the AI server order backlog actually gets more than straightened out. I updated my order backlog data on Dell after reviewing their Q1 earnings report, the Q1 earnings call, and the Q1 quarterly filing.

The first thing noticeable is the volume of AI server backlog of orders Dell sourced in its first quarter of FY26/CY25 alone. Dell’s backlog of AI server orders surged to $14.4B, almost single-handedly eclipsing the $16.2B in AI server orders it received in all of FY25/CY24.

The $14.4B in AI server orders Dell received was enough to propel Dell’s consolidated backlog out from the $38-39B the company was recording per quarter for the past 7 quarters. As of Q1 FY26, Dell now boasts of a consolidated backlog of $49B, which has sequentially grown 29% in Q1 and 26% y/y from the same period last year.

The surge in orders of Dell’s AI servers it received in Q1 coincides with multiple demand signals across the AI infrastructure landscape. For example, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) themselves reported on their Q1 call that the Blackwell ramp that they saw was “the fastest in our company's history.”

On Dell’s Q1 call, Dell’s management confirmed that the surge in orders was due mainly to Blackwell demand:

Well, the backlog is primarily Blackwell, and it has a combination of GB or ARM-based Blackwell with x86 Blackwell. It has some Hopper in it as well.

Even IDC reports that the growth in Q1 was much more than they expected. As a result, the technology research company has revised up their estimates for worldwide server growth, now estimating the server market to grow at an 18.8% CAGR versus prior estimates of 16.3% CAGR growth.

In my view, the unpredictability of Dell’s AI server orders that we saw over the past two quarters came from hyperscalers and other tech companies holding off for general availability of Nvidia’s Blackwell server systems. While hyperscalers were typically the leading indicators for demand signals across surging AI capex budgets, the demand signals have broadened out to a host of other players, such as AI startups like OpenAI and neoclouds such as CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) and Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS).

In fact, Dell, which is a leading server provider and partner within Nvidia’s protected ecosystem of infrastructure players, shares a unique vendor relationship with CoreWeave, another Nvidia ecosystem player. In November last year, Dell confirmed that CoreWeave would be the first recipient of the company’s liquid-cooled PowerEdge XE9712 integrated with Nvidia’s GB200 systems. CoreWeave's rapidly expanding capex budgets would benefit Dell.

A final point that I’d like to call out, which markets have surely missed, is the fact that Dell has gotten very efficient at shipping out its backlog to customers. In the Q1 quarterly filing, the company reported that 67% of the company’s backlog would be recorded over the next twelve months. That is a meaningful improvement over the percent of backlog that Dell would be recording as recognized revenue on an NTM basis. Perusing back through prior reports, investors can see that Dell would recognize ~60% of its remaining performance obligations, a metric for recording consolidated company backlog, over the next twelve months. With the robust demand and growing share of AI server backlog in the company’s consolidated backlog, the company is moving quickly to build & ship servers out to its customers, which indicates a faster path to revenue recognition versus its own history.

On the Q1 call, Dell’s management explained how they are attempting to ship out their backlog:

We're 91 days into the year with $12.1 billion of orders to date with a five-quarter pipeline that is significantly greater than the backlog of $14.4 billion. We're going to ship $7 billion of that backlog into Q2. And we're going to go try to convert, that's our job, convert the pipeline in Q2 and [indiscernible] second half into orders. Our best outlook is that, we're in a $15 billion plus range of shipped revenue.

At the current rate, management is expecting ~$15B in shipped server revenue.

So, even if the past quarterly revenue trends indicate that its ISG group revenues (Infrastructure Solutions Group) are sluggish, the surge in AI orders points to a robust recovery in ISG group revenues over the next 12 months. This recovery should be led by a reversal in shipments of AI servers, a metric that has been lagging per my observation in Exhibit A due to sluggish order activity through H2 of last year.

Dell Continues To Be Under-appreciated

With the surge in orders, as I noted in this post, I now believe Dell could score at least 9.5% growth in forward sales throughout the year. That is above my prior estimates of 8-9% growth, which markets appear to be now settled at.

Markets also seem overly concerned with Dell’s margin profile. I do understand the market's points of concern with Dell’s margins, especially since the company will be forced to pressure their margins as they rush to ship server orders and beat out a heavily competitive environment. On top of that, the high volume of inventory will also be impacting margins at the moment. Still, my estimates point to Dell’s ISG operating income to be slightly growing, versus some analysts who on the call had projected for ISG operating income to stay flattish. My assumption for this is that Dell will be in a slightly stronger margin position when accelerating server shipments to clients like CoreWeave, who have shown to be extremely aggressive with their capex plans. With Nvidia's Blackwell shipments expected to ramp through faster through the year, Dell's shipments of servers should benefit from the ripple effect.

In that scenario, Dell should be able to grow its earnings in the range of 15-16%, again marginally higher than the current estimates of ~15% growth. Also, current estimates have barely moved since the start of the year, down just 0.3%. However, I see that Dell's forward PE has compressed more versus the earnings outlook, which provides Dell’s stock enough room to trade up to the prior average of 15.1x.

With Dell currently trading at 13x forward earnings, the stock has ~16% upside in the stock with the assumption that the stock should trade at 15.1x forward earnings versus the current valuation of 13x forward earnings. That implies a PT of ~$143.4.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

Dell is currently in a marginal state of margin flux as it cycles through its record volumes of inventory. Much of that inventory also comes with the company holding server products with Hopper GPUs as well as PCs and other products sold within the CCG (Client and Consumer Group). On a TTM basis, I estimate that Dell’s inventory as a percent of revenue is elevated at 7.7% versus its 10-year historical average of ~4.2%, per my calculations. This is the other deflator that could pressure margins as Dell attempts to cycle through its inventory levels.

Add to this risk the tight competition in the server market, which is getting whipsawed by Foxconn’s growing relationship with Nvidia.

In my view, given where things currently stand, Dell is not the most exciting stock on the market, but given how markets are viewing the stock versus its risks, the prospect of Dell’s earnings growth potential is likely being downplayed, leaving meaningful room for upside.

Takeaway

Dell’s surging orders for AI server shipments is a strong sign that its server business is due to see an inflection over the next few quarters. Markets do not appear to be fully pricing in Dell’s growth potential due to some concerns about its margins and are downplaying the earnings growth potential.

Given the mismatch in growth expectations, I believe Dell is undervalued at forward earnings of 13x, and I reiterate my Buy rating on Dell.

data.title: Healthcare And The U.S. Economy
data.description:
The third quarter of 2025 is underway. So far, at least, it has seamlessly picked up the baton from Q2: quiet headline data that fails to make an impact on the Alfred E. Neuman (what, me worry?) market that we talked about in our commentary last week.

On Thursday, that headline data point was the monthly jobs report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Now, we might have expected this report to land on our desks with all the angst of an awkward middle schooler. Earlier this week, a different jobs report, the ADP Employment Survey, showed a net loss in hiring for June, with payrolls shrinking by 33,000 souls. Would the BLS report, where economists expected to see payroll gains of 117,500 and a slight uptick in unemployment to 4.3 percent, also deliver a surprise to the downside?

Nope. The Bureau reported a 147,000 increase in nonfarm payrolls and a small decrease in the unemployment rate to 4.1 percent. Given where we are in the economic cycle, that’s about as tidy a labor market snapshot as one could hope for.

All Roads Lead to Healthcare

Around a third of those nonfarm payroll gains are attributable to one single industry: healthcare. This is consistent with the trend thus far this year; in fact, healthcare is the nation’s top employer, growing from nine percent of the total workforce in 2000 to around 13 percent today.

Along with the increase in bodies employed as healthcare workers, their salaries have been rising as well. On average, since 1980, wages in healthcare have grown at nearly twice the rate of pay in the rest of the economy. And, in what will come as a surprise to absolutely nobody, American households now spend more annually on health-related expenses than they do on groceries or housing.

So it would not be inaccurate to say that as healthcare goes, so goes the US economy. Which brings us to the other bit of news this week: the tax cut and spending bill that is working its way through the House and has a very high likelihood of arriving on the president’s desk for signing roughly sometime not too long after this commentary appears in your inbox. There is plenty to be concerned about in this bill (to put it mildly), but let’s focus on the bits that pertain to healthcare.

Not Going to Age Well

The American population, like those in most other countries, is ageing. As the country gets older, access to affordable healthcare becomes more important. The bill that is about to become law, however, is expected to make steep cuts to Medicaid and other healthcare subsidies to the tune of around one trillion dollars over the next decade, resulting in somewhere between 12 to 17 million Americans losing their health insurance.

Medicaid, which primarily serves lower-income households, makes up around one sixth of total healthcare spending. The spending cuts are likely to weigh heavily on the operations of clinics, hospitals and other providers of services to low-income communities.

We expect this will show up, sooner or later, in the jobs numbers that healthcare’s fortunes have kept buoyant for so long. Unfortunately, it is also likely to show up in the statistics showing a country getting sicker as it ages.

The US economy has a history of being remarkably flexible in adapting to changing circumstances. When manufacturing jobs left the country in the 1990s and early 2000s, the growing healthcare industry was able to cushion the blow for anyone willing to trade in production-line skills for training in nursing, lab technician work and the like. We may be approaching another one of those junctures where new doors will have to open as others close. What lies on the other side of those doors, though, remains to be seen.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Modiv Industrial: A Mispriced Pure-Play In Mission-Critical Net-Lease
data.description:
About Modiv Industrial

Modiv Industrial (NYSE:MDV) is one of the more overlooked names in the REIT space—but it occupies a unique and attractive niche. After launching as Rich Uncles with a diversified real estate strategy, the company has executed a pivot to become the only publicly traded REIT focused solely on net-leased, mission-critical manufacturing facilities.

That repositioning now gives Modiv a distinct competitive profile. With macro tailwinds like U.S. onshoring, a shrinking supply of quality industrial assets, and strong tenant retention trends, Modiv’s portfolio is positioned to benefit from structural demand. At the same time, the company is continuing a successful derisking of its office exposure and is executing on a conservative, debt-conscious playbook.

What makes Modiv compelling for income-focused investors is its monthly dividend payout (the name Modiv literally stands for “monthly dividend”). For those reinvesting distributions, this frequency accelerates compounding and enhances long-term total returns—especially when starting from a compressed valuation. In a market where sentiment toward REITs remains cautious, Modiv’s consistent income stream adds a layer of predictability.

With a small market cap, high average cap rates, and long lease terms, Modiv offers asymmetric upside. Even a modest improvement in funds from operations (FFO) could translate into meaningful equity gains. If interest rates begin to ease over the next 12–24 months—and management continues reducing leverage, coupled with a dividend reinvestment plan or DRIP investing strategy, the stock has a credible path to double in value within five years, in fact, that is my base case.

Why Now

While near-term interest rate cuts remain unlikely, that may actually present the most compelling reason to look at Modiv today. The market has largely priced in a “higher for longer” scenario, which has weighed heavily on REITs—particularly small-cap names with leverage, which are often perceived as more vulnerable in a restrictive rate environment.

Modiv has not been spared in that selloff, despite the company delivering two consecutive FFO beats and making steady progress in deleveraging and repositioning its portfolio. The stock, at this writing, trades at just 9.6x forward FFO, a valuation that leaves little margin for credit to its execution and niche positioning.

Recent comments from President Trump provided signals that he would only appoint a Federal Reserve chair committed to lowering interest rates. While that outcome is hypothetical, even the prospect of policy change might serve as a beneficial tailwind for interest-rate-sensitive sectors like REITs.

In the meantime, Modiv's valuation today is fear-based, rather than fundamentals. For an early investor, the alignment of macro pressure, operating momentum, and severely discounted multiple creates a rare asymmetric chance.

Macro Tailwinds: U.S. Manufacturing Revival

U.S. manufacturing is enjoying a reversal driven by onshoring, geopolitical uncertainty, and fiscal incentives. As BlackRock has noted, global supply chains are redrawn for resilience—not just efficiency, demand for mission-critical domestic manufacturing facilities is growing.

  • Manufacture construction spending has grown 3x since January 2020 to $234 billion. This shows real and tangible investments into industrial infrastructure.
  • Over 750,000 new manufacturing jobs have been created since 2021, with a projected 3.8 million to be added by 2033.
  • U.S. policy is fully backing this shift. More than $2.1 trillion in federal spending (including the IRA, and CHIPS Act) plus $614 billion in private-sector commitments are fueling domestic manufacturing capacity.

For a REIT like Modiv, which focuses solely on net-leased, mission-critical manufacturing facilities, this renaissance is more than a trend—it’s a direct growth catalyst. Modiv’s portfolio sits at the intersection of this demand, with long-duration leases, high retention, and attractive cap rates. The reshoring wave isn’t a story for the future—it’s already being built today, and Modiv is positioned to capture the real estate tail end of that buildout.

Business Model: Precision Over Scale

Modiv Industrial has carved out a unique niche in the REIT world by being thoroughly mission-critical, single-tenant industrial buildings—buildings critical to the operations of their tenants on a daily basis. These are not generic warehouses or easily substituted logistics facilities. Rather, they are integral to the tenant’s manufacturing, production, or operational processes. Relocating them would be both financially burdensome and operationally disruptive.

This focus extends to tangible gains. Tenants tend to remain for a long period of time, and that's part of what is behind Modiv's consistent 98% occupancy rate and weighted average lease term (WALT) of 13.8 years, according to its latest 10-Q. Within the company's entire 44-property portfolio, that occupancy rate translates to just one vacancy—solid evidence of lease durability for a REIT of Modiv's size.

Modiv employs a triple-net-lease structure, in which the occupants are responsible for paying taxes, insurance, and maintenance. This structure provides the firm with steady base rent and modest operating expenses—a vehicle ideal for income-oriented investors.

The leases also contain rent escalators. A recent example: in Q1 2025, Modiv renewed a 10-year lease with Fujifilm Dimatix in Santa Clara, commanding 3% per annum rents plus an initial one-time rent reset of 6.75% to align rents to market levels. The escalators serve as an in-built growth driver, increasing Modiv's rents even during periods with fewer external acquisitions.

While Modiv has a fairly narrow portfolio, it is spread out geographically across more than 15 U.S. states, helping to contain exposure to the economic downturns in individual local markets.

Perhaps most typical is the company's straight-shooting approach with capital management. CEO Aaron Halfacre has mentioned on a podcast that he physically walks through each property before acquiring it—a level of due diligence uncommon at a publicly traded REIT. That philosophy was again on display in Q1 2025, when Modiv declined to buy any new properties even though there was dry powder that could have been used. The reason was not lack of opportunity but commitment to valuation discipline and ensuring that any purchase would be FFO-accretive over a three-year period.

To assess whether a deal adds value, management uses a three-year FFO accretion model—a longer term lens in a market that often prioritizes short-term NOI growth. Even modest annual increases in FFO per property can significantly move the needle, given Modiv’s small size. This creates meaningful upside potential from well-timed, disciplined additions.

Careful Capital Allocation

Modiv’s approach to capital deployment remains disciplined in a market filled with noise. On the surface, rising cap rates should present buying opportunities—after all, you’re paying less for every dollar of income. But the math only works if your cost of capital hasn’t risen in tandem.

That’s the challenge today: while marketed cap rates have ticked up, so has Modiv’s cost of equity—now hovering between 14% and 17%, according to management. That narrows the spread and reduces the accretive impact to FFO. At the same time, the quality of assets coming to market has declined, with many being private equity-led sales—properties offloaded by private equity firms looking for liquidity, not long-term alignment. As CEO Aaron Halfacre put it, “Those motivations are not always in line with us as a long-term holder.” Still, Modiv has shown in the past that it’s willing to act when the math makes sense. In 2023, as cap rates widened and private equity purchasers retreated, the company took advantage of the improved climate, buying at more attractive yields and with fewer competitors—proving capable of being opportunistic, but patient when the time is right.

Modiv continues to take a cautious approach with its at-the-market (ATM) equity program, balancing the need for incremental capital with the goal of protecting shareholder value. I appreciate that management is highly mindful of dilution— the CEO noted, “I don’t like issuing even at 16 necessarily, because I think that’s a steep discount.” While they do recognize the need to gradually increase float to improve liquidity and reduce volatility, they’re clearly weighing the tradeoff between issuing equity and the financing it could provide to pursue new acquisitions. In my view, this level of restraint—only raising capital when pricing and deal quality align—is a positive signal for long-term investors

Modiv has also been buying back its Series A Preferred Stock at a discount, a prudent move that is beneficial to common stockholders. By buying back 205,000 of its Series A Preferred Stock, the company is able to grow AFFO available to common stockholders, improve its dividend safety score and unlocking dividend growth. This is a low-cost, high-return investment of capital in a market where there are not compelling acquisition opportunities. This is an indication of a management team that is disciplined and shareholder- oriented.

Insider Skin in the Game

More than 8.5% of Modiv shares are owned by insiders, a heavy stake that reconciles managerial and shareholder interests. The CEO has been continuously buying shares—with a string of open-market purchases in June 2025, demonstrating confidence in the long-term value of the company amidst broader REIT headwinds. This sort of insider buying, especially in small-cap name Modiv, is generally an extremely bullish sign that the management thinks the price is disconnected with intrinsic value. For investors, that faith generates an extra sense of security.

Risks To Watch

Although Modiv has clearly progressed in its portfolio tightening and capital restraint, there are several risks investors should bear in mind. From tenant concentration to rising interest rates, these factors may impact both near-term results and longer-term trajectory unless handled judiciously with further prudence and thoughtful strategy.

1. Tenant Concentration

While Modiv has a diversified 29-tenant base in 43 properties, there is meat to the concentration within its top two: Lindsay Corp. and a Kia auto dealership together represent 24% of gross rent. Both tenants are looking healthy financially—Lindsay has no net debt, and Kia is backed by its parent—but this remains a material risk if either tenant is vacated. Long lease terms reduce near-term exposure. However, the bigger picture isn't credit—it's strategic fit for the KIA dealership. To their credit, Modiv announced during Q2 2024 that they will recycle the Kia dealership capital into core industrial assets—showing an understanding that the property is outside of their long-term strategic orbit.

2. Execution Risk on Non-Core Dispositions

As Modiv continues on its path of having a pure-play industrial platform, it still holds four non-core retail and office properties as of December 2024. The management is aware of such exposure and is working towards managing it down, as and when market opportunities allow. Worth mentioning, however, is that some of these non-core properties make material contribution to revenue. That shows the need for disciplined execution, not to disrupt cash flows.

3. Interest Rate Sensitivity:

Rising interest rates have pushed the cost of capital for Modiv higher, which has narrowed the margin on acquisitions. That's made it harder to find deals that build real value for shareholders. Unless the prices are adjusted or really attractive opportunities present themselves, outside growth will likely be muted. While this is a prudent strategy in today's times, it could hinder near term growth.

4. Tenant Credit Risk

Approximately 33% of Modiv's tenants possess an investment-grade credit rating, which means that 67% of the tenants are unrated and thus entail a certain level of risk, as a high percentage of the portfolio is exposed to potential credit risk—especially during uncertain economic times.

5. Re-Leasing Challenges

Modiv's industrial buildings are generally built to suit a specific tenant or use, and hence they become increasingly hard to re-lease if a tenant unexpectedly vacates. These properties are often located in secondary or logistics-heavy locations—not high-demand locations—limiting buyer markets and reconversion options. That stickiness equals long and costly downtimes, producing yet another risk factor in long-term cash flow stability.

Using the DRIP to Double Your Investment

Bearing in mind Modiv's conservative allocation of capital, growth in FFO, and clear focus on core industrial assets, a conservative growth projection for share price is 7-10% annually over the next 5 years.

Assuming low multiple expansion as the portfolio moves out of non-core holdings and interest rate easing. And regarding the dividend, management hiked it earlier this year and has teased further hikes based on AFFO growth—so a 4-5% dividend CAGR would not be unreasonable (given less preferred shares outstanding). Inputting these assumptions into a dividend reinvestment calculator yields a total return of 104%, which is my base case. That would be a 2x over 5 years if my macro thesis for falling rates comes to fruition and management continues to do its thing.

Conclusion

Modiv Industrial is a specialized REIT that is trading at a deep discount, with improving fundamentals and a concentrated and shareholder-friendly management. Its mission-critical manufacturing portfolio stands to benefit directly from robust macro reshoring trends, while diligent capital allocation limits downside. With a market cap of just $170 million, even modest FFO growth or multiple re-rating could translate into outsized returns. I rate Modiv a Buy—with risk—but one that creates an unusual asymmetric scenario for long-term investors who are willing to get in early.
